coredata.dc:description: In recent years, the widespread use of sulfonamide antibiotics has led to increasing concerns over antibiotic residues. To address the challenges, this study developed a rapid and highly sensitive detection method based on ratiometric fluorescence. By employing nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-BCDs) and biomass-derived carbon quantum dots (RCDs), single-channel and ratiometric fluorescence sensors were constructed. The incorporation of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) enhanced the specificity and sensitivity of target recognition. Experimental results demonstrated that the ratiometric fluorescence sensor exhibited superior anti-interference capability and detection stability compared to the single-channel sensor, achieving a detection limit as low as 10.41 nM, significantly outperforming the single-channel sensor's limit of 25.94 nM. To enable rapid on-site detection, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor was integrated with a microfluidic paper-based chip, resulting in a visual detection platform. Moreover, a microfluidic paper-based chip with a spatially rotational structure was developed, integrating smartphone photo-recognition functionality to convert fluorescence ratio changes into RGB values, enabling rapid visual detection of SDZ. In practical applications involving milk and water samples, the sensor achieved recovery rates between 98.70 % and 108.70 %, demonstrating excellent performance. This ratiometric fluorescence microfluidic paper-based chip sensor offers simplicity, high sensitivity, and strong selectivity, making it suitable for non-professional users to perform rapid on-site detection. It shows great potential for applications in food safety and environmental monitoring.

head.ce:title: A rapid microfluidic paper-based chip sensor using ratiometric fluorescence and molecularly imprinted polymers for visual detection of sulfadiazine in actual samples

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, the widespread use of sulfonamide antibiotics has led to increasing concerns over antibiotic residues. To address the challenges, this study developed a rapid and highly sensitive detection method based on ratiometric fluorescence. By employing nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-BCDs) and biomass-derived carbon quantum dots (RCDs), single-channel and ratiometric fluorescence sensors were constructed. The incorporation of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) enhanced the specificity and sensitivity of target recognition. Experimental results demonstrated that the ratiometric fluorescence sensor exhibited superior anti-interference capability and detection stability compared to the single-channel sensor, achieving a detection limit as low as 10.41 nM, significantly outperforming the single-channel sensor's limit of 25.94 nM. To enable rapid on-site detection, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor was integrated with a microfluidic paper-based chip, resulting in a visual detection platform. Moreover, a microfluidic paper-based chip with a spatially rotational structure was developed, integrating smartphone photo-recognition functionality to convert fluorescence ratio changes into RGB values, enabling rapid visual detection of SDZ. In practical applications involving milk and water samples, the sensor achieved recovery rates between 98.70 % and 108.70 %, demonstrating excellent performance. This ratiometric fluorescence microfluidic paper-based chip sensor offers simplicity, high sensitivity, and strong selectivity, making it suitable for non-professional users to perform rapid on-site detection. It shows great potential for applications in food safety and environmental monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, with the growth of the global population and the intensification of agricultural production, sulfonamide antibiotics have been widely applied in livestock farming, with sulfadiazine being commonly used due to its effective antibacterial properties for the prevention and treatment of bacterial infections in animals (Caniça et al., 2019). Among them, sulfadiazine (SDZ), due to its effective antibacterial properties, is widely used in animal breeding to prevent and treat bacterial infections (Baran et al., 2011). However, the misuse and overuse of sulfonamides in animals have led to the accumulation of antibiotic residues in food products, particularly in milk, meat, and seafood (Sarmah et al., 2006). These residual antibiotics can enter the human body through the food chain, potentially causing health issues such as antibiotic resistance, allergic reactions, and long-term toxic accumulation. Furthermore, the release of sulfonamide residues into natural water bodies via agricultural wastewater discharge might also pose potential threats to the balance of aquatic ecosystems (Loghin et al., 2020; Tasho and Cho, 2016). The seriousness of these issues highlights the critical need for the development of detection technologies for sulfonamide residues.

Traditional methods for detecting antibiotic residues in complex food matrices often face interference from fats, proteins, and minerals. New approaches must be highly sensitive, minimize matrix interference, and be simple enough for non-professionals to enable rapid on-site detection. This is essential for real-time monitoring of residues in the food supply chain to protect consumer health. While methods like HPLC and GC-MS provide high sensitivity and accuracy, they require costly equipment, complex sample preparation, and trained operators, making them impractical for on-site use. (Arabi et al., 2022; Moudgil et al., 2018; Patyra et al., 2018; Qin et al., 2022). Thus, developing a highly sensitive, selective, and convenient method for the rapid detection of SDZ residues in complex matrices, such as milk and water, is of great practical significance.

Fluorescence sensors have recently emerged as a novel tool for food safety detection. By utilizing the luminescent properties of fluorescent molecules, they enable quantitative analysis through signal changes in the presence of target substances. Compared to traditional methods, fluorescence sensors offer greater sensitivity, faster response, and easier operation (Karacaglar et al., 2020). Particularly, carbon quantum dots (CQDs)-based fluorescence sensors, owing to their excellent optical properties, such as strong fluorescence emission, good photostability, and resistance to photobleaching, have become core materials for fluorescence sensor construction (Long et al., 2021). Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-BCDs) and biomass carbon quantum dots (RCDs) exhibit outstanding performance in enhancing fluorescence emission intensity and improving fluorescence quantum yield (Alaghmandfard et al., 2021; Lu et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2023). By introducing nitrogen atoms or utilizing biomass resources to synthesize RCDs, these materials significantly enhance optical performance and environmental friendliness, demonstrating broad application prospects. Ratiometric fluorescence sensors improve upon traditional fluorescence sensors by detecting changes in the ratio of two fluorescence signals, enabling accurate quantitative analysis. They combine high sensitivity and rapid response with enhanced stability and reliability, effectively reducing environmental interference to ensure consistent and accurate detection (Dai et al., 2023; Zhu et al., 2022). To further improve sensor performance, researchers have fine-tuned the fluorescence emission peaks and intensities of CQDs through surface modification and structural regulation, optimizing control over the ratiometric fluorescence signals. Additionally, with dual-channel detection, ratiometric fluorescence sensors can improve the accuracy of visual detection through changes in fluorescence color.

Simultaneously, molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) serve as highly specific and selective artificial recognition materials, offering greater potential for designing ratiometric fluorescence sensors (Arabi et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2016). MIPs form highly matched three-dimensional recognition sites during polymerization, enabling precise recognition and capture of specific molecules (Dai et al., 2023; Ostovan et al., 2022; Zhu et al., 2022). The integration of MIPs with CQDs ensures highly specific and sensitive fluorescence-based detection of sulfadiazine in complex matrices (Wang et al., 2024). Although the integration of MIPs with CQDs offers superior selectivity and sensitivity, the portability and efficiency of the sensor remain key areas for further optimization.

To enhance the portability and efficiency of the detection system, the introduction of microfluidic paper-based chip technology offers a novel solution to these challenges. Innovative 3D microfluidic paper-based chips are emerging as essential tools in food safety and environmental monitoring due to their low cost and strong portability (Qin et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2022). Microfluidic paper-based chips enable precise liquid control in microchannels, allowing for integrated processing of complex chemical analyses. Combined with ratiometric fluorescence sensors and MIPs, these chips accelerate detection while significantly simplifying sample preparation and handling, making them ideal for rapid on-site detection (Li et al., 2021; Qin et al., 2022).

This study developed a ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on CQDs and MIPs, integrated with a microfluidic paper-based chip, for the rapid, sensitive, and visual detection of SDZ residues. The fluorescence emission characteristics of CQDs were utilized to design and optimize the sensor, achieving high-sensitivity detection of SDZ by modulating the fluorescence behavior of CQDs and incorporating the highly selective recognition function of MIPs. By measuring the ratio of different fluorescence signals, the sensor effectively minimized environmental interference, enhancing detection accuracy and reliability. The integration with a microfluidic paper-based chip streamlined the detection process, offering a simplified workflow for sample preparation and enabling efficient on-site detection. This innovative approach not only provided a robust solution for the rapid detection of sulfadiazine residues but also demonstrated significant potential for broader applications in actual samples.

Corn stalks and leaves (locally sourced), citric acid monohydrate (CA), urea (UR), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), ammonia (25 %), methanol (99.5 %), acetic acid, anhydrous ethanol (99.5 %), sodium hydroxide, disodium hydrogen phosphate, and sodium dihydrogen phosphate were purchased from China National Pharmaceutical Group Corporation. Sulfadiazine (SDZ), sulfamethazine (SM), sulfamethoxazole (SM2), sulfamethoxazole (SMX), sulfadimethoxine (SDM), Alkyl Ketene Dimer (AKD), n-Heptane, 1-(4-phenylazo-phenylazo)-2-naphthol (Sudan Ⅲ) were provided by Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

Fluorescence spectra were measured using an FS5 spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK); UV–visible absorption spectra were detected using a UV-2700 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan); Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was conducted using a Nicolet iS50 instrument (Thermo Fisher, USA); X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed using a D8 ADVANCE diffractometer (Bruker AXS, Germany); X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained with an AX spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan); morphology and structural observations were made with an SU8100 scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi, Japan) and a JEOL 2100 PLUS transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, Japan); and the hydrophobic layer of the paper chip was printed using a Canon TS3380 inkjet printer (Canon, China).

The synthesis of N-BCDs was adapted from a previously reported method (Lai et al., 2020). Citric acid (3.274 g) and urea (6.362 g) were dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water, sonicated until fully dissolved, and transferred to a PTFE-lined autoclave. The reaction was carried out at 180°C for 6 h. After the reaction, the solution was cooled to room temperature and filtered twice. The filtered solution was centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 8 min to remove unreacted large particles, and the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane. Finally, the resulting quantum dot solution was dialyzed using a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 24 h to obtain purified N-BCDs.

The RCDs were synthesized using ultrasound-assisted ethanol extraction. 8 g of fresh corn stalks and leaves were cleaned with deionized water and soaked in 100 mL of deionized water. The solution was then transferred to a PTFE-lined autoclave and reacted at 160°C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solid residue was filtered, washed with deionized water three times, and then treated with ethanol by ultrasonic extraction for 1 h. After soaking for an additional 26 h, RCDs were obtained.

The synthesis of N-BCDs@MIPs was modified based on a previous method (Wang, Wen, Chen, et al., 2022). First, 3 mL of N-BCDs and 2.013 mL of TEOS were dissolved in 50 mL of ethanol, sonicated until fully dissolved, and stirred for 10 min. Then, 50 mg of SDZ was dissolved in 10 mL of aqueous solution, to which 400 μL of 0.2 M NaOH solution was added, followed by 1.122 mL of APTES. This mixture was stirred for 1 h. Afterward, 1 mL of ammonia was added, and the mixture was stirred for an additional 20 h, resulting in a milky solution. After the reaction, the solution was centrifuged at 8800 rpm for 8 min, washed alternately with ethanol and water four times, and dried for 15 h. The dried solid was subjected to Soxhlet extraction with a methanol/acetic acid (9:1, v/v) solution to remove SDZ. The resulting product was vacuum-dried to obtain N-BCDs@MIPs. N-BCDs@NIPs were synthesized in a similar way, but without the addition of the template molecule SDZ. The synthesized products were dried and collected as solids without an elution step.

To prepare the ratiometric fluorescence sensing system for SDZ detection, 100 μL of 2 mg/mL N-BCDs@MIPs solution was mixed with 30 μL of RCDs solution in a 2 mL centrifuge tube. The system volume was adjusted to 350 μL and diluted with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.0).

During fluorescence measurements, 2 mg/mL suspensions of N-BCDs@MIPs/RCDs were prepared. SDZ standard solutions of varying concentrations were added, and the samples were sonicated at room temperature for 20 min to ensure full reaction. Fluorescence spectra were recorded in the 370–800 nm range using an excitation wavelength of 335 nm, a photomultiplier tube voltage of 700 V, and 3 nm excitation/emission slit widths. Each experiment was repeated three times. For comparison, N-BCDs@NIPs/RCDs, N-BCDs@MIPs, and N-BCDs@NIPs were tested under the same conditions. Selectivity was assessed by introducing structural analogs and interfering ions at concentrations 100 times higher than that of the analyte.

First, the open-source software Excalidraw was used to design the rotating microfluidic paper chip (Figure S1), which consists of three layers: upper, middle, and lower. Modifications were made based on reference (Qi et al., 2018), ensuring that the area of each of the three detection zones in the paper chip was 78.5 mm2, guaranteeing consistent fluid flow across all areas. The hydrophilic channel on the second layer has a width of 2.8 mm, allowing smooth fluid passage. Both the first and second layers feature three observation holes, allowing direct observation by rotating the chip to align the desired hole after detection.

The hydrophobic layer was prepared using an inkjet printer (Canon TS3380), which was used to print self-made ink, which was a mixture composed of AKD, n-heptane, and Sudan Ⅲ in a specified ratio, multiple times onto Whatman No. 1 filter paper attached to a 200 × 200 mm substrate. The concentration of AKD was adjusted according to reference (Deng et al., 2021), using a concentration of 26 mg/mL in this experiment. After printing, the filter paper was heated in an oven at 100 ℃ for 5 min to form the first hydrophobic barrier. After naturally cooling to room temperature, wax was sprayed onto the printed paper chip to give the three layers different colors. The chip was then placed in an oven at 60 ℃ for 3 min, allowing the wax to permeate through the paper and form the second hydrophobic barrier. 5 μL of N-BCDs@MIPs/RCDs solution was applied to each of the three detection areas on the μPAD, with the addition process repeated five times to achieve a uniform coating. After allowing the solution to dry, the rotary MIPs-μPAD fluorescence sensor was ready for use.

To evaluate the practical applicability of the N-BCDs@MIPs/RCDs fluorescence sensor, commercially available milk and Yangtze River water (Zhenjiang section, Jiangsu Province, China) were selected as actual samples for spiked recovery tests with varying concentrations of SDZ (10, 20, 30 μmol/L). Milk samples were diluted with PBS buffer, followed by the addition of 10 % trichloroacetic acid for centrifugation. Water samples were first filtered to remove suspended particles, mixed with methanol in a 1:1 (v/v) ratio, and similarly treated with 10 % trichloroacetic acid for centrifugation. For both sample types, the supernatant was collected and filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to remove residual proteins or fine particles, yielding pretreated samples. The pretreated samples were spiked with standard SDZ solutions, and three parallel experiments were conducted to evaluate detection performance.

Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis (Fig. 1I) indicates a typical type IV isotherm, suggesting that the MIPs exhibit an ordered mesoporous structure. Table S2 shows that the specific surface area of N-BCDs@MIPs is 12.47 m²/g, which provides a high binding capacity. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns (Fig. 1D) revealed a broad peak at 2θ = 23.3° for N-BCDs@MIPs, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphite carbon, further confirming the layered graphite structure of N-BCDs. The presence of this graphite structure enhanced the material's electron transfer ability and fluorescence intensity, improving its sensing performance.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) elemental data (Fig. 1E) composition table (Table S1) indicated that N-BCDs@MIPs were mainly composed of Si (24.20 %), O (52.74 %), N (3.86 %), and C (19.20 %). This result aligned with the N1s XPS spectrum (Figure S3), where a peak at 401.1 eV corresponds to the C–N bond, further confirming the successful incorporation of nitrogen atoms and the successful preparation of N-doped BCDs. Elemental mapping analysis (Fig. 1C1–C4) showed the distribution of Si, O, C, and N, consistent with the XPS elemental composition and the EDS results (Fig. 1 F). Specifically, the EDS results showed that N-BCDs@MIPs consist of C (24.82 %), N (7.17 %), O (42.17 %), and Si (25.84 %), further validating that the surface of N-BCDs had been successfully modified by the imprinting layer.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy tests (Figure 1 G) further confirmed that the N-BCDs showed a broad peak of O-H/N-H at 3451.2 cm-¹ , indicating their good hydrophilicity (Wang et al., 2020). The prominent CC stretching vibration of the aromatic rings appears at 1640.9 cm−1 (Yang et al., 2024). Additionally, the peak at 1396.3 cm−1 in N-BCDs corresponds to the C-N stretching vibration, indicating successful incorporation of nitrogen atoms into the N-BCDs. The distinct C-O-C peak at 1115.9 cm−1 further confirmed its enhanced solubility in water.

The FT-IR spectra of MIPs and NIPs (Fig. 1H) showed the asymmetric stretching vibration of the C-H bond in silane at 2935.8 cm⁻¹ (Tian et al., 2021), as well as Si-O-Si (1085.3 and 791.6 cm⁻¹) and Si-O (461.6 cm⁻¹) groups (Wang, Wen, Zhao, et al., 2022), indicating the presence of APTES and TEOS. The characterization of MIPs before and after elution (Fig. 1H) shows that the characteristic absorption peak of SDZ was observed near 1543 cm−1 disappears after elution, confirming the successful removal of the template molecule and successful synthesis of the imprinted polymer. Systematic FT-IR analysis confirmed the successful incorporation of N-BCDs and the synthesis of MIPs and NIPs, while also demonstrating the effectiveness of molecular imprinting in recognizing the template molecules.

Extensive studies on the luminescent properties of the synthesized materials had validated their feasibility for constructing ratiometric fluorescence sensors. As shown in Figure S4A, N-BCDs exhibited a broad emission peak at 430 nm under excitation at 335 nm. The introduction of nitrogen atoms effectively modulated the electronic distribution in carbon quantum dots, significantly enhancing their fluorescence efficiency by increasing active sites (Hu et al., 2023). This optimization allowed N-BCDs to produce intense blue fluorescence emission even under low excitation energy, making them suitable for sensor construction.

Fig. 2A showed that the pre-synthesis N-BCDs emit bright blue fluorescence, while post-synthesis, the fluorescence intensity slightly decreases. This phenomenon might be due to the MIPs layer partially shielding the luminescent sites on the surface of N-BCDs during synthesis, thereby slightly reducing the fluorescence intensity. However, this decrease did not significantly affect its potential as a core luminescent material in ratiometric fluorescence sensors. Even after the MIPs coating, N-BCDs still maintained their highly efficient fluorescence emission properties.

Fig. 2B illustrated the emission peaks of N-BCDs@MIPs and N-BCDs@MIPs/RCDs at 430 nm, further verifying that the nanosenor retained the characteristic fluorescent properties of N-BCDs. By incorporating RCDs, a stable dual-channel ratiometric fluorescence system was established. Notably, the stable red emission signal of RCDs at 675 nm served as a reliable external reference signal for the sensor, significantly improving the accuracy and anti-interference ability of fluorescence detection.

As shown in Figure S4B, after constructing the ratiometric fluorescence sensor, the optimal excitation wavelength of N-BCDs remained 335 nm, indicating that the optical properties of N-BCDs were not significantly affected in the sensor system. By analyzing the fluorescence intensity ratio
F
430
/
F
675
, we could effectively monitor the fluorescence signal changes, where
F
430
represented the blue emission intensity of N-BCDs, and
F
675
represents the red emission intensity of RCDs. This ratio was used to quantify the fluorescence response, particularly in the nanoparticle ratiometric sensor system, where changed in the ratio clearly reflect the concentration of the target molecule (e.g., SDZ). To evaluate the practical application potential of the nanosensor, SDZ was introduced for testing. As shown in Fig. 2C, both N-BCDs@MIPs and N-BCDs@NIPs exhibit fluorescence quenching at 430 nm upon the addition of SDZ. Notably, MIPs demonstrate a more significant fluorescence quenching effect, attributed to the specific recognition sites of MIPs selectively capturing SDZ molecules, resulting in an enhanced quenching effect. Therefore, N-BCDs@MIPs could serve as a sensitive signal indicator suitable for SDZ detection. In contrast, the red emission of RCDs at 675 nm remained almost unchanges, making it an ideal reference signal, further proving the feasibility of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor (N-BCDs@MIPs/RCDs) for SDZ detection.

Furthermore, studies on the fluorescence quenching mechanism suggest that fluorescence quenching between SDZ and N-BCDs might occur via an electron transfer mechanism. As shown in Figure S4C, the UV–visible absorption spectrum of SDZ showed little overlap with the emission spectrum of N-BCDs, further proving that the quenching effect was mainly caused by electron transfer rather than energy transfer. Meanwhile, upon binding with MIPs, a blue shift appears in the absorption spectrum, indicating changes in electron energy levels, thereby enhancing the fluorescence quenching effect. This mechanism confirms that the fluorescence quenching phenomenon induced by SDZ was through electron transfer.

To further enhance the performance of the N-BCDs@MIPs/RCDs fluorescence sensor, key detection conditions were systematically optimized. These parameters included the molar ratio of the target molecule to functional monomers, the optimal concentration of N-BCDs@MIPs, elution time, solution pH, and sensor response time. These optimization experiments ensured that the sensor exhibited optimal sensitivity and stability in practical detection, maximizing its ability to detect SDZ.

First, to determine the optimal molar ratio of the target molecule to functional monomers, absorbance experiments were conducted with different concentrations of SDZ (Figure S4A). When the SDZ concentration reached 80 μM, the absorbance was at a suitable level, and thus this concentration was chosen for subsequent optimization of the functional monomer ratio. Solutions with varying molar ratios of SDZ to functional monomers from 1:0–1:8 were prepared, with the 1:0 group serving as a blank control. The results showed a gradual redshift in the SDZ absorbance peak as the functional monomer ratio increased. The maximum redshift occurred when the SDZ to functional monomer ratio was 1:6 (Figure S4B), making this the optimal ratio for SDZ recognition. Subsequently, the polymerization time was optimized by comparing the
F
0
/
F
values of MIPs at different polymerization times, with 20 h determined to be the optimal synthesis time (Table S3). Next, the elution time of the MIPs was optimized to ensure the complete removal of the template molecule. Fresh eluent was used for each experiment to guarantee effective removal. As shown in Figure S4C, the SDZ residue in the eluent decreased over time. After 24 h, the SDZ concentration in the eluent reached a low level, indicating that the template molecules had been mostly removed. Thus, 24 h was selected as the optimal elution time for the MIPs to ensure no residual interference in subsequent detections.

When determining the optimal concentration of N-BCDs@MIPs, it was important to balance between maintaining a wide linear range and ensuring high signal sensitivity. Various concentrations of N-BCDs@MIPs were tested, with 2.0 mg/mL identified as the optimal concentration (Figure S4D), which ensured a wide linear range and high detection sensitivity. The solution pH, evaluated across a range of 2–11, significantly influenced the sensor's fluorescence response, with the intensity peaking under neutral conditions and declining at higher or lower levels (Fig. 2D). Introducing SDZ confirmed that this environment provided the highest binding efficiency, minimal signal fluctuations (Fig. 2E), and the most stable and reliable detection performance, establishing pH 7.0 as the optimal working condition for the sensor. The sensor’s response time was optimized by monitoring the fluorescence ratio
F
430
/
F
675
over time. As shown in Fig. 2F, the ratio decreased rapidly and stabilized, indicating that the binding between N-BCDs@MIPs and SDZ reached equilibrium within 14 minutes, which was selected as the optimal response time for practical detection applications.

Under the optimized detection conditions, the fluorescence signal at 430 nm of the system decreased with increasing SDZ concentration ( Fig. 3). In the sensor performance analysis, the standard curve method and linear regression analysis were used to quantitatively assess the sensitivity of the sensor to SDZ detection (Table S4). The fluorescence emission characteristics of each system were further analyzed to elucidate the sensitivity and linear detection range of the constructed sensors.

In the single-channel fluorescence sensing system (Fig. 3A and 3B), the blue fluorescence emission intensity of the N-BCDs@MIPs sensor at 430 nm (
F
430
) gradually decreases with increasing SDZ concentration, indicating a more pronounced fluorescence quenching effect as the concentration of the target molecule increases. Particularly within the concentration range of 0–100 μM, N-BCDs@MIPs exhibit a more significant fluorescence quenching effect compared to N-BCDs@NIPs. This was due to the presence of highly specific recognition sites for SDZ in the N-BCDs@MIPs. This phenomenon demonstrates the high selectivity and sensitivity of the MIPs layer in the sensing process. The degree of fluorescence quenching (
F

/
F
) and SDZ concentration could be described by the Stern-Volmer equation (Gehlen, 2020).

When only N-BCDs@MIPs were used as signaling probes (Fig. 3A), fluorescence intensity (

When N-BCDs@MIPs and RCDs were combined to form a ratiometric fluorescence sensor (Fig. 3D), it showed that the response signal (

As shown in Fig. 3F, the N-BCDs@MIPs/RCDs ratiometric fluorescent sensor exhibits a more sensitive response with respect to N-BCDs@NIPs/RCDs. In addition, the N-BCDs@NIPs/RCDs-based the linear slope ratio of MIPs and NIPs in the ratiometric sensing system, the calculated imprinting factor was 12.25, which was larger than that in the single-emission sensing system (9.40), further verifying the critical role of the molecular recognition ability of MIPs in the sensor (Table S7). This dual-channel sensor significantly improved the detection accuracy, especially in the detection of low concentration of the target, and its sensitivity was much higher than that of the single-emission sensing system. The LOD of N-BCDs@MIPs/RCDs was as low as 10.41 nM, which shows that the present study outperforms most of the SDZ detection strategies ( Table 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, stability, and anti-interference ability analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the sensor’s performance in practical applications, its selectivity, anti-interference ability, and stability were thoroughly analyzed. The sensor's response to SDZ and structurally similar antibiotics, such as SDM, SM, SMZ, and SMX, was tested. Despite shared structural similarities ( Fig. 4A), the single-emission fluorescence system showed significant fluorescence changes only in the presence of SDZ, with minimal interference from other antibiotics or their mixtures (Fig. 4B). This excellent selectivity is attributed to the specific recognition sites in the MIPs, which are tailored to match the spatial structure and chemical properties of SDZ.

The sensor also demonstrated robust anti-interference capability. In the presence of common interfering ions like Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, and SO4 2-, it maintained accurate SDZ detection with significant fluorescence changes (Fig. 4C). This resilience is due to the protective silica coating and the precise molecular recognition of MIPs, which shield the fluorescence center from environmental interference.

Stability tests confirmed the sensor's suitability for long-term use. Over a 2-hour period, the single-emission fluorescence signal remained stable under continuous excitation (Fig. 4D). Similarly, in ratiometric fluorescence detection, the
F
430
/
F
675
ratio remained unaffected by 100 μM of interfering ions or substances, enabling accurate detection of 60 μM SDZ in complex matrices such as milk and water (Fig. 4E and F). This improved stability and detection reliability in challenging environments stem from the combined effects of specific recognition sites and the silica coating layer.

Long-term testing further demonstrated the ratiometric system’s robustness, with the fluorescence ratio
F
430
/
F
675
remaining stable over extended detection periods, even in complex matrices like milk and water (Fig. 4G). The integration of RCDs contributed significantly to this stability, ensuring precise and reliable SDZ detection in real-world applications.

The sensor's performance relied on a fluorescence quenching mechanism, where interactions between a quencher and fluorescent molecules reduce fluorescence intensity. Here, SDZ served as the quencher, causing fluorescence quenching in N-BCDs via electron transfer. In the static quenching mechanism, SDZ molecules interact with N-BCDs to form a non-fluorescent complex, preventing effective excitation of the fluorescence center and causing quenching. Experimental data (Fig. 3A) show a significant decrease in fluorescence intensity with increasing SDZ concentration. The linear correlation between quenching and SDZ concentration highlights the efficient capture of SDZ by the specific recognition sites of MIPs, enhancing the quenching effect. In a dynamic quenching mechanism, the quenching effect was triggered by electron transfer between SDZ and N-BCDs. The UV-Vis absorption spectrum of SDZ showed minimal overlap with the fluorescence emission spectrum of N-BCDs (Figure S4C), indicating that energy transfer was not the primary quenching pathway. Instead, electron transfer might occur between SDZ molecules and the surface of N-BCDs, where SDZ acts as an electron acceptor and captures electrons from the excited state of N-BCDs, leading to a decrease in fluorescence intensity. This electron transfer mechanism explains the observed decrease in fluorescence intensity as the SDZ concentration increases.

The fluorescence quenching and SDZ concentration exhibited a highly linear relationship in the single-channel fluorescence system, with a regression coefficient of 0.99441, indicating strong correlation in the 0–100 μM concentration range. The quenching constant was calculated to be 0.01326 μM⁻¹ , showing a strong quenching effect between SDZ and N-BCDs. The detection limit of N-BCDs@MIPs was determined to be 25.94 nM, significantly lower than that of N-BCDs@NIPs, further confirming that MIPs enhance the sensitivity for SDZ detection through molecular recognition. In the ratiometric fluorescence sensing system, the fluorescence ratio also exhibited a highly linear relationship with SDZ concentration, with a regression coefficient of 0.99504. The detection limit for this system was as low as 10.41 nM, significantly improving sensitivity and accuracy compared to the single-channel system.

In neutral aqueous solutions, the silanol groups (Si-OH) on the siloxane surface partially dissociate to form negatively charged siloxane ions (SiO-), resulting in a negative zeta potential for the MIP. Upon binding with SDZ molecules at the specific recognition sites of the MIP, the surface charge density may change, leading to a shift in zeta potential. This binding could also subtly redistribute functional groups and alter the surface structure, further influencing the zeta potential (Figure S7).

To evaluate the practicality of the sensor, we selected commercial milk and water from as real samples and conducted recovery experiments by spiking SDZ into each. For the milk sample, pretreatment effectively minimized interference from proteins and other macromolecular components, allowing the sensor to focus specifically on detecting sulfadiazine antibiotics. The water sample was also pretreated to remove large particulate matter. These steps ensured the accuracy and reliability of the detection process. By adding various concentrations of SDZ to the pretreated samples, recovery rates ranged from 98.7 % to 108.7 % (Table S8), demonstrating the sensor’s excellent reliability for SDZ detection in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection performance of the microfluidic paper chip
ce:section.ce:para: While the ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor based on N-BCDs@MIPs/RCDs showed excellent sensitivity for SDZ detection, the need for conventional large instruments might not meet the requirements for on-site, real-time detection. Therefore, to satisfy the need for accurate on-site monitoring of SDZ, a portable and smart sensing platform was designed and developed.

This portable detection device consists of a black box with an upper shooting hole (Figure S6A) and a portable 365 nm UV lamp placed inside the box (Figure S6B). The assembled microfluidic paper chip was then placed under the 365 nm UV lamp (Figure S6C), and a smartphone was used to capture images through the shooting hole for RGB analysis (Figure S6D). Upon introducing the fluorescence sensor with different concentrations of SDZ, as shown in Fig. 5A, a clear color change was observed under 365 nm UV light, which corresponds to the CIE chromaticity coordinates (Fig. 5B). As shown in Fig. 5C, the color recognition was performed by using a smartphone to take a photo, and the RGB value of the photo color was analyzed for comparative analysis based on the performed point taking (Table S9), which eliminated the error caused by visual deviation.

The rotary microfluidic paper chip was left to stand for about 15 min after adding the assay liquid and was rotated clockwise to the observation hole position by the white “knob” in Fig. 5D, avoiding the step of disassembling the microfluidic paper chip device. Subsequently, the microfluidic paper chip was rotated to the observation hole position and placed into a portable camera box, and the color changes detected on the paper chip were photographed (Fig. 5E). The RGB values obtained from the microfluidic paper chip were then compared with Fig. 5C to verify the effectiveness of visual detection. Although slight differences in fluorescence color between the liquid and paper chip might exist, the RGB values correspond to specific concentrations within an allowable error range.

This proposed sensing system, combining the microfluidic paper chip with smartphone imaging, allowed for on-site quantitative monitoring of SDZ. The integration of a rotating microfluidic paper chip and a smartphone camera enabled fast and portable detection, providing a reliable and user-friendly platform for antibiotic residue monitoring.

In this study, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on N-BCDs and biomass RCDs was successfully developed. By integrating MIPs and a microfluidic paper-based chip, the system enabled rapid, sensitive, and visual detection of SDZ residues in milk samples. The sensor exhibited excellent selectivity and sensitivity in complex matrices, owing to the modulation of the fluorescence emission behavior of N-BCDs and the specific recognition capability of MIPs. The ratiometric fluorescence sensor achieved a detection limit as low as 10.41 nM, significantly improving the detection performance compared to the single-channel fluorescence sensor with a detection limit of 25.94 nM. In practical sample detection, the sensor demonstrated recovery rates between 98.70 % and 108.70 %, indicating its high practicality. Furthermore, the integration of a rotating microfluidic paper chip and smartphone-based image recognition allowed for visual, quantitative detection of SDZ, providing an efficient and convenient solution for on-site monitoring of antibiotic residues in food safety applications. This system not only offered high sensitivity and simple operation but was also suitable for non-professional users to perform rapid on-site detection, showcasing broad application potential.
